## One mega-chat vs. many smaller chats — strong recommendation: **many**

| | One long chat | Multiple chats per phase |
|---|---|---|
| Coherence | "Automatic" — but degrades fast | Has to be engineered (easy, see below) |
| Agent quality | Drops sharply once context fills up; Cursor starts truncating/summarizing silently — your later prompts get dumber answers | Each chat starts with full context budget |
| Reviewer experience | One giant 200KB blob — hard to evaluate "phases" | Clear named files = clear narrative of how you worked |
| Matches the rubric? | Risky — they explicitly ask logs for **every phase** (framing, research, planning, coding, docs, testing, QA) — easier to demonstrate with separate files | Yes — and the rubric literally says "switch or combine [agents]" is fine |

**Verdict:** one chat per phase. Not one chat per file-edit — that's overkill. Roughly 6–10 total chats over 24–48 hours.

## How you maintain coherence across separate chats

The trick: **the JSONL is the *record*, not the *memory*.** Memory lives in artifacts that persist between chats. Five mechanisms, layered:

1. **The git repo itself** — every new chat opens with the same codebase. The agent can `git log` and read the actual code to know "where we are."
2. **`docs/decisions.md`** — you (and the agent) append to it as you go. Any new chat reads it first → instant context on every decision so far.
3. **`docs/domain-model.md`** — same idea, but for the model. It's your single source of truth.
4. **`AGENTS.md` at the repo root** — persistent project-level instructions Cursor auto-loads into every new chat in this workspace. This is where you'd put: "We chose Python FastAPI + React. Coverage rules are JSON-configured. State machine lives in `app/states.py`." etc.
5. **Commit messages** — write them like a narrative. `feat: add LineItem state machine (submitted → adjudicated → paid)`. They become a free changelog the next chat reads.

Concretely, every new chat would start with a 1-line prompt like:

> "Read `AGENTS.md`, `docs/decisions.md`, and the last 10 commits. Then [today's task]."

That gives the next chat ~95% of the context the previous chat had, without burning any of its window on rebuilding it.

Let me explain the mechanics clearly first — I should have done that upfront. I just verified there's no global `~/.cursor/AGENTS.md` on your machine, so this matters.

## How Cursor actually loads AGENTS.md

**AGENTS.md is workspace-scoped.** Cursor only auto-loads the AGENTS.md at the root of the *currently open* workspace. So:

- When you open `new-here/` → Cursor reads `new-here/AGENTS.md`. ✅
- When you open `claim-evaluator-bot/` → Cursor reads `claim-evaluator-bot/AGENTS.md`. ❌ It does **not** also read `new-here/AGENTS.md`.

Even though you wrote your `new-here/AGENTS.md` to be "general" in spirit, Cursor has no idea it's meant to be cross-project. It just reads whatever file is in the current workspace root.

There's also no `include` / `import` mechanism in AGENTS.md — it's plain markdown, not a programmable config. You can't write "also load that other file" inside it.

## So your two real options

| | Option A: Inline (one file) | Option B: Project-only (one file, slim) |
|---|---|---|
| What goes in `claim-evaluator-bot/AGENTS.md` | Project-specific stuff **+ a copy** of your general rules | Project-specific stuff only |
| Where do general rules live? | Inside this file (copy of `new-here/AGENTS.md`) | Only in `new-here/AGENTS.md` — which **Cursor won't load here** |
| Does the agent actually follow your general rules in this workspace? | **Yes** | **No** — the agent only sees the slim project file |
| What the reviewer sees in the zip | Complete, self-contained AGENTS.md | A slim AGENTS.md missing your safety rails |
| Maintenance | If you ever update general rules, you copy-paste to each project | Single source of truth in `new-here/` (but it doesn't apply anywhere else anyway) |

**Option B is what I was offering, but on reflection it's basically broken** — your general rules wouldn't actually be active in this workspace, which defeats the point. I should have framed it more bluntly.

## My recommendation: Option A, one file, two layers

```text
claim-evaluator-bot/
└── AGENTS.md     ← single file
    ├── [project-specific section on top]
    └── [your general rules pasted below]
```

This is exactly the pattern your own `new-here/AGENTS.md` already anticipates ("If a project adds its own AGENTS.md section above these rules, the project-specific guidance wins on conflict."). You were already planning for this.

## Bonus: one-time setup so you stop re-pasting

If you want, after this project I can make your `new-here/AGENTS.md` truly global by moving it to `~/.cursor/AGENTS.md` (or `~/.cursor/rules/general.mdc`) — Cursor *does* auto-load those across all workspaces. But that's a separate cleanup; skip it for now.

## Doing it now

Going to:
1. Read your `new-here/AGENTS.md` again to copy the general rules exactly.
2. Create `claim-evaluator-bot/AGENTS.md` with project-specific layer on top + your general rules pasted below.
3. The project-specific layer will include the "one-liner you paste in every new chat" baked right into the file.

# AGENTS.md

> **Project-specific guidance is at the top. General behavioral rules are
> at the bottom. On conflict, project-specific wins.**

---

## Onboarding for a new chat (READ FIRST)

Whenever a chat in this workspace starts, do this before anything else:

1. Read this entire `AGENTS.md`.
2. Read `docs/decisions.md` — the running log of what was built, what was
   skipped, and the reasoning behind each call. This is the single source
   of truth for "where we are."
3. Read `docs/domain-model.md` — entities, relationships, state machines.
4. Run `git log --oneline -20` to see the recent commit narrative.
5. Only then start on the task the user asked for.

The user's one-liner to paste in any new chat is:

> "Read `AGENTS.md`, `docs/decisions.md`, `docs/domain-model.md`, and
> `git log --oneline -20`. Then: <today's task>."

---

## Project context

**What this is:** a take-home assignment to build a working **Claims
Processing System** for an insurance company. The deliverable is a
runnable system that ingests claims, adjudicates them against coverage
rules, tracks them through lifecycle states, and explains every decision.

**Time budget:** 24-48 hours total.

**Submission must include** (per the rubric):

- `app/` — backend application code
- `frontend/` — web UI
- `docs/domain-model.md` — entities, relationships, state machines
- `docs/decisions.md` — what was built, what was skipped, assumptions
- `docs/self-review.md` — honest assessment
- `ai-artifacts/` — **raw `.jsonl` Cursor session logs covering every
  phase** (framing, research, planning, coding, docs, testing, QA).
  Missing JSONLs = auto-reject.
- `README.md` — setup and run instructions
- `.git/` — full commit history (no single-commit dumps)

**Out of scope** (explicitly per the spec — do not build):

- Auth, login, registration
- Policy purchase / enrollment flows
- Member / provider account management
- Email notifications
- Analytics dashboards
- Admin panels
- Multi-tenancy or RBAC

---

## Stack

| Layer | Choice | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Backend language | Python 3.11+ | Type hints required |
| Backend framework | FastAPI | REST API serving the frontend |
| Package manager | `uv` | Fast, modern, lockfile-based |
| Persistence | SQLite + SQLAlchemy | File-based, zero setup for reviewer |
| Backend testing | `pytest` | Tests encode domain behavior, not just HTTP codes |
| Frontend | Vite + React + TypeScript | Strict TS |
| Frontend testing | Vitest + React Testing Library | Only if time permits — backend tests come first |
| Interface | Single-page web UI | Talks to the FastAPI backend over REST |

---

## Repo layout (target — will fill in as we scaffold)

```text
claim-evaluator-bot/
├── app/                       # Python backend (FastAPI)
│   ├── api/                   # Route handlers
│   ├── domain/                # Pure domain logic (entities, rules, state machines)
│   ├── adjudication/          # Coverage-rule engine
│   ├── persistence/           # SQLAlchemy models + repos
│   ├── main.py                # FastAPI app entrypoint
│   └── tests/                 # pytest suite
├── frontend/                  # React + TS UI
│   ├── src/
│   ├── package.json
│   └── vite.config.ts
├── docs/
│   ├── domain-model.md
│   ├── decisions.md
│   └── self-review.md
├── ai-artifacts/              # Raw JSONL session logs (copied at the end)
├── data/                      # Sample policies, claims, seed data
├── AGENTS.md                  # This file
├── README.md
├── pyproject.toml             # uv-managed Python project
└── .git/
```

---

## Project-specific conventions

### Domain modeling

- Domain logic lives in `app/domain/`. It must be **pure** — no DB
  imports, no FastAPI imports. The domain should be testable with plain
  Python objects.
- Persistence (`app/persistence/`) and API (`app/api/`) are thin
  adapters around the domain.
- Coverage rules are **data, not code** — represented as structured
  configuration the rules engine interprets. Reason: business rules
  change often, code shouldn't be the place rules live.

### State machines

- Both **claims** and **line items** have lifecycles. Model both
  separately. A claim's state is derived from its line items' states,
  not stored independently as a source of truth that can drift.
- Every state transition must produce an audit-log entry with: who/what
  triggered it, before/after state, timestamp, reason.

### Explanations

- Every adjudication decision (approve / deny / partial / needs review)
  must carry a structured explanation: the rule(s) that fired, the
  inputs that were checked, the amount math. The UI surfaces this; the
  API returns it as part of the line item response.

### Tests

- Tests appear **in the same commit as or before** the code they cover.
  The git history is reviewed for this — do not bolt tests on at the
  end.
- Test names describe behavior, not implementation:
  - Good: `test_line_item_denied_when_service_type_not_covered`
  - Bad: `test_adjudicate_returns_200`
- Domain logic tests live in `app/tests/domain/` and never touch the DB
  or the HTTP layer.

### Commits

- Conventional-commit-ish prefixes: `feat:`, `fix:`, `refactor:`,
  `test:`, `docs:`, `chore:`.
- One logical change per commit. The history is read by the reviewer
  as the story of how this was built.
- Commit messages should explain **why** more than **what** when the
  what isn't obvious from the diff.

### Documentation discipline

- `docs/decisions.md` is **append-only during the project**. Every
  non-trivial decision gets a dated entry with: context, options
  considered, choice, reasoning. This is how a new chat catches up.
- `docs/domain-model.md` evolves with the model and reflects the
  current state.
- `docs/self-review.md` is written near the end. Be honest about gaps.

### AI session logs

- Cursor JSONLs live at
  `~/.cursor/projects/Users-adagrewal-Desktop-Sumer-stuff-claim-evaluator-bot/agent-transcripts/<uuid>/<uuid>.jsonl`.
- We do **one chat per phase**. Naming convention when copying into
  `ai-artifacts/` at the end: `NN-phase-name__<uuid>.jsonl` (e.g.
  `03-planning__619c8af9-...jsonl`). Keep the UUID so the file is
  obviously the raw original.
- Phases (target list):
  1. `01-framing` — understanding the assignment
  2. `02-domain-research` — insurance / adjudication research
  3. `03-planning` — architecture, schema, state machines
  4. `04-scaffolding` — repo layout, deps, AGENTS.md
  5. `05-backend-core` — entities, persistence
  6. `06-backend-adjudication` — rules engine, state machine
  7. `07-frontend` — React UI
  8. `08-tests` — finalize test coverage
  9. `09-docs` — fill out the three docs
  10. `10-qa` — bug hunt, polish, edge cases

---

## How to run (will fill in once scaffolded)

```bash
# Backend
cd app
uv sync
uv run uvicorn main:app --reload

# Frontend (in another terminal)
cd frontend
npm install
npm run dev
```

(README.md will mirror this with full setup steps for the reviewer.)

---

## Phase tracker

- [x] **01-framing** — assignment understood, deliverables mapped
- [ ] **02-domain-research**
- [ ] **03-planning**
- [ ] **04-scaffolding**
- [ ] **05-backend-core**
- [ ] **06-backend-adjudication**
- [ ] **07-frontend**
- [ ] **08-tests**
- [ ] **09-docs**
- [ ] **10-qa**

Update this list at the end of each phase.

---

# General behavioral rules

> These are cross-project defaults — they should apply regardless of
> language, framework, or repo. The project-specific guidance above
> wins on conflict.

## Safety & version control

- Never commit, amend, push, or run destructive git commands unless I
  explicitly ask.
- Never modify git config or rewrite history.
- Don't open pull requests on my behalf unless I ask.
- Don't commit files that may contain secrets (`.env`, credentials, tokens).

## File & change discipline

- Always read a file (or the relevant section) before editing it. Don't
  blind-edit based on a guess about its current contents. Re-read if you're
  unsure, even when you "remember" the file from earlier in the session.
- Prefer editing existing files over creating new ones.
- Don't create documentation, README, or summary files unless I ask.
- Keep diffs minimal and focused on the task. No drive-by refactors or
  reformatting of code I didn't ask you to touch.
- Don't add narrating comments (e.g. `// increment counter`). Comments
  should only explain non-obvious intent or constraints.

## Communication

- Be concise. Skip preamble like "Great question!" and post-amble summaries
  of what you just did unless I ask.
- No emojis unless I ask.
- If you're unsure between a few approaches, ask before going down a path
  that requires significant code changes.

## Completion & honesty

- If a task isn't fully done, something is broken, or you skipped a step,
  say so explicitly at the end. Don't claim success when there are known
  failures, untested paths, or TODOs you left behind.
- A clear "this works but I didn't test X" or "I implemented A but B is
  still failing" is always better than implying everything is fine.
- Don't paper over errors by catching and ignoring them, hardcoding
  return values, or commenting out failing assertions just to make
  things "work".

## Planning

- For non-trivial tasks (more than ~3 steps or touching multiple files),
  briefly outline the plan before editing.
- If the task is ambiguous or has meaningful trade-offs, ask clarifying
  questions instead of guessing.

## Dependencies

- Don't add new dependencies for trivial reasons.
- When you do add one, use the package manager to pick a real current
  version - never invent or guess versions.
- Prefer the existing package manager already used in the repo.

## Code quality

- Match the existing style and conventions of the file/project you're in.
  Don't impose preferences from other projects.
- Use type hints / type annotations in any language that supports them
  (TS strict, Python with type hints, etc.) for new code.
- After substantive edits, check for and fix any linter errors you
  introduced. Leave pre-existing lints alone unless I ask.
- Add logging at meaningful boundaries, especially in long or multi-step
  workflows (agent pipelines, background jobs, async tasks, retries).
  Log inputs/outputs of each major step, errors with context, and any
  state transitions that would be needed to debug a failed run after the
  fact. Use the project's existing logger / log format - don't `print()`
  into production code paths.

## Tools & shell

- Use specialized tools (read/edit/search) for file operations rather
  than `cat`/`sed`/`awk`/`grep` in the shell.
- Don't run long-lived shell commands without confirming.

## Destructive operations

- Never run destructive data or system operations without explicit
  confirmation from me, even when they seem implied by the task. This
  includes: database drops/truncates, schema migrations, mass row
  deletes or updates without a `WHERE` clause, `rm -rf` on real paths,
  force pushes, history rewrites, and bulk file deletions.
- If a task seems to require one of the above, stop and ask first,
  describing exactly what will be affected.

## Scope discipline

- Do what was asked. If you notice unrelated issues, mention them at the
  end instead of fixing them inline.
- Don't expand the task into "while we're here, let's also..." without
  asking first.
- If my request seems incoherent, internally contradictory, or unclear
  (conflicting requirements, vague scope, ambiguous target, reasoning
  that doesn't follow), stop and ask clarifying questions before
  implementing anything. Don't try to guess my intent or pick the
  "most likely" interpretation on a non-trivial task.

# Decisions & Trade-offs

> Running, append-only log of non-trivial decisions made during the
> project. Every entry: context, options considered, choice, reasoning.
> A new chat reads this file to catch up on where the project stands.

---

## 2026-06-23 — Stack: Python + FastAPI + uv (backend), React + TS + Vite (frontend), SQLite + SQLAlchemy

**Context:** The assignment requires a backend that processes claims and a
single interface to demonstrate the flows. The spec leaves stack open.

**Options considered:**

| Backend | Why considered | Why not |
|---|---|---|
| FastAPI + uv | Fast dev loop, strict typing via Pydantic, modern Python tooling, OpenAPI for free | — |
| Flask | Lighter, more familiar | Weaker typing story, more boilerplate for validation |
| Django | Full ORM + admin out of the box | Overkill for 24-48 hr scope; admin is explicitly out of scope |

| Frontend | Why considered | Why not |
|---|---|---|
| Vite + React + TS | Fastest dev server, type safety mirrors backend Pydantic models | — |
| Next.js | SSR, routing built in | SSR not needed for this scope; adds complexity |
| Plain HTML templates from FastAPI | Zero JS toolchain | Wanted a proper SPA to show off interactive flows |

| Persistence | Why considered | Why not |
|---|---|---|
| SQLite + SQLAlchemy | Real DB, file lives in repo, zero setup for reviewer | — |
| Postgres in Docker | More production-realistic | Adds Docker dependency for the reviewer; not justified for scope |
| In-memory dicts | Simplest | No persistence across restarts; can't model audit log realistically |

**Choice:** FastAPI + uv for backend, React + TS + Vite for frontend,
SQLite + SQLAlchemy for persistence.

**Reasoning:** This stack maximizes type safety end-to-end (Pydantic ↔
TypeScript), keeps reviewer setup to "clone, install, run" with no
Docker or external services, and matches the time budget.

---

## 2026-06-23 — Interface: single-page web UI

**Context:** The spec says "pick one" of REST API, web UI, or CLI.

**Choice:** Web UI (React SPA) backed by a FastAPI REST API.

**Reasoning:** A UI is the most demonstrable interface for a non-technical
reviewer to walk through claim submission, adjudication, and disputes. The
REST API exists underneath anyway and is the actual contract; the UI just
exercises it.

---

## 2026-06-23 — Repo layout: split `app/` (backend) and `frontend/` at root

**Context:** Three layout shapes were possible: (1) backend at root with
templated HTML, (2) backend in `app/` + frontend in `frontend/`, both at
repo root, (3) monorepo with `packages/backend` and `packages/frontend`.

**Choice:** Option 2.

**Reasoning:** Matches the spec's `app/` requirement exactly while
keeping the JS toolchain isolated in `frontend/`. No monorepo
ceremony needed for two-package scope.

---

## 2026-06-23 — AGENTS.md strategy: self-contained file in repo root

**Context:** Maintaining coherence across multiple Cursor chats (one
per phase) requires a persistent context layer. Cursor auto-loads
`AGENTS.md` from the workspace root, but does not load files from
sibling workspaces.

**Choice:** Single `AGENTS.md` at the repo root, containing
project-specific guidance on top and general behavioral rules
(copied from the user's cross-project template) below.

**Reasoning:** Self-contained means the file ships with the repo and
the reviewer sees a complete artifact. Project-specific rules win on
conflict per the convention already established in the user's
cross-project template.

---

## 2026-06-23 — One Cursor chat per phase, JSONL-linked by phase prefix

**Context:** The rubric mandates raw `.jsonl` session logs covering
every phase (framing, research, planning, coding, docs, testing, QA).
Submissions without complete JSONLs are auto-rejected.

**Options considered:**

1. **One mega-chat for everything** — automatic coherence but Cursor
   context degrades; reviewer sees one giant unstructured blob.
2. **One chat per phase** — engineered coherence (via AGENTS.md +
   `docs/decisions.md` + git history); reviewer sees a phase-labeled
   trail.

**Choice:** Option 2. Ten phases planned (see AGENTS.md "Phase
tracker"). At submission time, JSONLs are copied from
`~/.cursor/projects/.../agent-transcripts/<uuid>/<uuid>.jsonl` into
`ai-artifacts/` and renamed `NN-phase-name__<uuid>.jsonl` so the
phase ordering is obvious while preserving the original UUID as proof
that the file is unmodified.

**Reasoning:** Cleaner agent context per chat, clearer phase narrative
for the reviewer, and the coherence problem is fully solved by
persisted artifacts (AGENTS.md + docs + commits).

# claim-evaluator-bot

A claims processing system for an insurance company. Members submit
claims with line items; the system adjudicates each line item against
coverage rules, moves the claim through its lifecycle, and explains
every decision.

Built as a take-home assignment. See [`docs/decisions.md`](docs/decisions.md)
for the reasoning behind every non-trivial choice.

## Status

🏗️ Foundation only. No application code yet. See the
[phase tracker in `AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md#phase-tracker) for progress.

## Stack

| Layer | Choice |
|---|---|
| Backend | Python 3.11+, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, Pydantic |
| Package manager | [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) |
| Persistence | SQLite (file-based, zero external setup) |
| Backend testing | `pytest` + `httpx` |
| Frontend | React + TypeScript, Vite |
| Frontend testing | Vitest + React Testing Library (if time permits) |

## Repo layout

```text
claim-evaluator-bot/
├── app/                       # Python backend (FastAPI)
│   ├── api/                   # Route handlers (thin HTTP layer)
│   ├── domain/                # Pure domain logic (entities, state machines) — no DB, no HTTP
│   ├── adjudication/          # Coverage-rule engine
│   ├── persistence/           # SQLAlchemy models + repositories
│   └── tests/                 # pytest suite (domain tests isolated from DB/HTTP)
│       └── domain/
├── frontend/                  # React + TS + Vite SPA (scaffolded in phase 07)
├── data/                      # Sample policies, claims, seed data
├── docs/
│   ├── domain-model.md        # Entities, relationships, state machines
│   ├── decisions.md           # Running log of decisions & trade-offs
│   └── self-review.md         # Honest assessment of what's good vs. rough
├── ai-artifacts/              # Raw Cursor JSONL session logs (copied at the end, one per phase)
├── AGENTS.md                  # Agent guidance: project context + general rules
├── README.md
├── pyproject.toml             # uv-managed Python project
├── .python-version            # 3.11
└── .gitignore
```

## Prerequisites

- Python ≥ 3.11
- [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/) (`brew install uv` or `pipx install uv`)
- Node.js ≥ 20 and `npm` (for the frontend)

## Setup

```bash
git clone <this-repo>
cd claim-evaluator-bot

# Backend
uv sync

# Frontend (once scaffolded in phase 07)
```

> **Note:** `uv sync` currently installs nothing because dependencies

```bash
# Frontend — in a separate terminal
```

> Both commands will be functional once their respective phases land

## Running tests


## Documentation

- [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) — agent instructions, phase tracker, and the

- [`docs/domain-model.md`](docs/domain-model.md) — entities,

Foundation is in place. Nothing committed yet — sitting in the working tree waiting for your sign-off. Here's the walkthrough on the three things you wanted to review.

---

## 1) Skeleton & folder purposes

Current tree (18 files, all new except the modified `README.md`):

```text
claim-evaluator-bot/
├── .git/                          (untouched)
├── .gitignore                     ← Python + Node + macOS ignore rules
├── .python-version                ← pins to 3.11 (uv reads this)
├── AGENTS.md                      ← from the previous turn
├── README.md                      ← rewritten (was a 21-byte stub)
├── pyproject.toml                 ← uv-managed Python project, deps empty for now
│
├── app/                           ← Python backend
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── api/__init__.py            ← Route handlers (thin HTTP layer)
│   ├── domain/__init__.py         ← Pure domain logic (no DB, no HTTP imports)
│   ├── adjudication/__init__.py   ← Coverage-rule engine
│   ├── persistence/__init__.py    ← SQLAlchemy models + repos
│   └── tests/
│       ├── __init__.py            ← pytest suite root
│       └── domain/__init__.py     ← Domain tests isolated from DB/HTTP
│
├── frontend/                      ← React + TS SPA (scaffolded in phase 07; empty for now)
│   └── .gitkeep
│
├── data/                          ← Sample policies, claims, seed data
│   └── .gitkeep
│
├── docs/
│   ├── domain-model.md            ← Stub (filled in phase 03)
│   ├── decisions.md               ← Already has 5 entries — our foundation decisions
│   └── self-review.md             ← Stub (filled in phase 10)
│
└── ai-artifacts/                  ← Raw JSONLs copied here at the end
    └── .gitkeep
```

**The non-obvious design call: separating `app/domain/`, `app/adjudication/`, `app/api/`, `app/persistence/`.** This isn't four folders for the sake of it — it enforces a one-way dependency rule:

```text
api ──► adjudication ──► domain
        ▲
persistence ──► domain
```

`domain/` is pure Python — no FastAPI, no SQLAlchemy imports. That means:
- Domain tests are blazing fast and don't need a DB or HTTP client.
- We can swap SQLite for Postgres later by only touching `persistence/`.
- The rules engine in `adjudication/` is testable with plain Python objects.

The rubric explicitly grades on **domain decomposition** and **rule representation**, so this structure is intentional signal.

**`__init__.py` vs `.gitkeep`:** Python packages need `__init__.py` files anyway (imports break without them), so they double as the "track this empty folder" marker. For non-Python folders (`frontend/`, `data/`, `ai-artifacts/`) I used `.gitkeep`. Both will be replaced by real content as we go.

**Files outside any folder, at the root:**

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pyproject.toml` | Declares the Python project. **Deps list is empty on purpose** — I refuse to invent version pins per your `AGENTS.md` rule. Phase 05 runs `uv add fastapi sqlalchemy ...` to install with real resolved versions and write the lockfile. |
| `.python-version` | Tells uv / pyenv to use Python 3.11. One line: `3.11`. |
| `.gitignore` | Standard ignore rules (venv, `__pycache__`, `node_modules`, `.DS_Store`, `*.db`, etc.). |

---

## 2) How AGENTS.md rules are handled

This is the coherence layer between chats. Three mechanisms working together:

| Layer | What it does | Where it lives |
|---|---|---|
| **Auto-load** | Cursor auto-loads `AGENTS.md` at the workspace root into **every** chat in this repo. No action needed by you. | `claim-evaluator-bot/AGENTS.md` |
| **One-liner trigger** | At the top of the file, an explicit ordered onboarding list ("read this, then `docs/decisions.md`, then `docs/domain-model.md`, then `git log -20`"). Your one-liner prompt in any new chat just says *"do the onboarding, then [today's task]"*. | `AGENTS.md` § "Onboarding for a new chat" |
| **Living state** | The agent doesn't just need rules — it needs to know **what's already been decided**. `docs/decisions.md` is the running ledger. Already pre-populated with 5 entries from this chat: stack, interface, repo layout, AGENTS.md strategy, JSONL-per-phase strategy. Any new chat reads it and is instantly caught up. | `docs/decisions.md` |

**Structure inside `AGENTS.md`** (lines 1–211):
- Lines 1–25: **Onboarding** ("READ FIRST" block) + the one-liner template
- Lines 26–63: **Project context** — what we're building, time budget, in-scope vs out-of-scope (copy-pasted from spec so agent can't drift)
- Lines 64–80: **Stack table**
- Lines 81–115: **Repo layout** (target — matches what we just built)
- Lines 116–185: **Project-specific conventions** — domain purity, coverage rules as data, audit trail on every state transition, tests appear in same/earlier commit as code, commit prefixes, JSONL naming convention, phase list
- Lines 186–211: **Phase tracker** with checkboxes (phase 01 marked done)
- Lines 212–319: **General behavioral rules** — your `new-here/AGENTS.md` content pasted verbatim, with a one-line preface saying project-specific wins on conflict

So when a new chat starts, Cursor sees:
1. Project rules first → it knows the stack, the conventions, the phase
2. General rules second → it knows the safety rails (no auto-commits, ask before destructive ops, etc.)
3. The onboarding block tells it which other files to read for current state

Your one-liner to paste in any new chat (also baked into `AGENTS.md`):

> "Read AGENTS.md, docs/decisions.md, docs/domain-model.md, and git log --oneline -20. Then: <today's task>."

---

## 3) README walkthrough

Rewrote the 21-byte stub into a proper README. Structure:

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| Title + one-paragraph pitch | What the system does. Points to `docs/decisions.md` for reasoning. |
| **Status** | "Foundation only. No application code yet." With a link to the phase tracker. Sets reviewer expectations honestly. |
| **Stack** | Table mirroring the AGENTS.md choices. |
| **Repo layout** | Annotated tree — same as what we just built, with one-line purpose per folder. |
| **Prerequisites** | Python ≥ 3.11, uv, Node ≥ 20. |
| **Setup** | `git clone`, `uv sync`, `cd frontend && npm install`. With an honest note that `uv sync` currently installs nothing because deps land in phase 05. |
| **Running** | `uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload` + `npm run dev`. Marked as "will be functional once their phases land". |
| **Running tests** | `uv run pytest` |

## Naming convention

When copying the JSONLs in at the end of the project, prefix each with
its phase number and name. **Keep the original UUID** in the filename so
it is obviously the raw original, not a relabeled or curated version.

```
...
```

## Phase list

1. `01-framing` — understanding the assignment, deliverables, foundation
2. `02-domain-research` — insurance / adjudication / coverage rules research
3. `03-planning` — architecture, schema, state machines
4. `04-scaffolding` — repo layout, dependency init

Logs are copied in **at the end of the project**, not per-phase, so

# Decisions

A running log of non-trivial decisions made during the build. Each entry
captures the **context**, the **options considered**, the **choice**, and
the **reasoning**. Append-only during the project; do not edit past
entries.

This doc is the single source of truth a new chat should read to catch
up on "where we are and why."

---

## 2026-06-23 — Stack: Python + FastAPI + uv (backend), Vite + React + TS (frontend), SQLite + SQLAlchemy

**Context:** Spec requires a runnable system with one interface. Stack
is unspecified — candidate's choice.

**Options considered:**

- Backend framework: FastAPI vs Flask vs Django.
- Package manager: `uv` vs `pip` + `venv`.
- Frontend: Vite + React + TS vs Next.js vs plain JS.
- Persistence: SQLite + SQLAlchemy vs Postgres in Docker vs in-memory.

**Choice:** FastAPI + `uv` for backend, Vite + React + TS for frontend,
SQLite + SQLAlchemy for persistence.

**Reasoning:**

- FastAPI gives type-checked request/response models and OpenAPI docs
  for free — useful when the reviewer is exploring the API.
- `uv` is fast and produces a reproducible lockfile so the reviewer's
  setup is deterministic.
- SQLite has zero install footprint — the reviewer doesn't need Docker
  or a running server. The DB file is gitignored; we seed on startup.
- Vite + React + TS is the fastest path to a working UI with type
  safety. Next.js's SSR/routing buys nothing for a claims-processing
  tool.

---

## 2026-06-23 — AGENTS.md as the coherence layer across chats

**Context:** Using multiple Cursor chats (one per phase) to keep JSONL
session logs cleanly separated per phase. Risk: each chat starts with
no memory of prior chats.

**Options considered:**

- One mega-chat for the whole project (no coherence problem, but agent
  quality degrades as context fills).
- Multiple chats per phase, coherence rebuilt via a shared artifact.

**Choice:** Multiple chats. `AGENTS.md` at the repo root acts as the
persistent context layer. Every new chat starts by reading `AGENTS.md`,
this `decisions.md`, `docs/domain-model.md`, and recent commits.

**Reasoning:**

- Cursor auto-loads `AGENTS.md` from the workspace root into every chat
  in this workspace — so the "read this first" instruction is enforced
  by tooling, not memory.
- Multiple chats keep each phase's JSONL log clean and easy for the
  reviewer to read in order.
- The rubric explicitly allows multiple agents/chats and expects
  per-phase logs.

---

## 2026-06-23 — Repo layout: domain layer kept pure, no DB or HTTP imports

**Context:** Need to decide where business logic vs persistence vs API
glue lives.

**Choice:** Three layers under `app/`:

- `app/domain/` — pure entities, value objects, state machines. No
  SQLAlchemy, no FastAPI imports allowed.
- `app/persistence/` — SQLAlchemy models + repositories.
- `app/api/` — FastAPI route handlers.
- `app/adjudication/` — the coverage-rule engine. Also pure — operates
  on domain objects, not DB models.

**Reasoning:**

- The domain layer becomes testable with plain Python objects — fast
  tests, no fixtures, no migrations.
- Forces explicit translation at the persistence/API boundaries, which
  catches a lot of accidental coupling early.
- Makes the rules engine swappable: if coverage rules ever moved to a
  DSL or external service, only the adjudication module changes.

---

## 2026-06-23 — Coverage rules represented as data, not code

**Context:** "How do you represent coverage rules?" is one of the
core questions in the spec.

**Options considered:**

- Hardcoded Python functions per rule type.
- Data-driven: rules live as structured records (JSON in seed files,
  rows in DB) that a generic rules engine interprets.
- Full DSL (overkill for 24-48 hrs).

**Choice:** Data-driven. Coverage rules are structured records the
adjudication engine reads and applies. Hardcoded fallback only for the
small handful of rule *kinds* (e.g. "annual limit," "per-visit cap,"
"requires preauthorization"), not for the specific values.

**Reasoning:**

- Real-world coverage rules change constantly per plan year and per
  policy. Code is the wrong place for them.
- Lets the demo show different policies producing different outcomes
  from the same engine — a key signal of good rule representation.
- Keeps the engine small and the rule catalog inspectable.

Doesn't affect correctness; just heads-up that not 100% of the diff is from me. I added the one missing `app/tests/api/__init__.py` to make it consistent and removed a redundant empty `ai-artifacts/.gitkeep` (since the folder already has a real README). --- ## 1. Folder structure — what each one is for ```text claim-evaluator-bot/ ├── app/ ← Python backend lives here │ ├── api/ ← FastAPI route handlers. Thin layer. │ │ Takes HTTP requests, calls into domain, returns JSON. │ ├── domain/ ← Pure business logic. NO DB imports. NO FastAPI imports. │ │ Claims, line items, policies, state machines. │ │ Testable with plain Python objects. │ ├── adjudication/ ← The coverage-rule engine. │ │ Reads rules (data), applies them to line items, │ │ produces decisions + structured explanations. │ ├── persistence/ ← SQLAlchemy models + repositories. │ │ Translates between domain objects and DB rows. │ └── tests/ ← pytest suite │ ├── domain/ ← Pure unit tests on domain logic (fast, no DB). │ └── api/ ← Integration tests through HTTP (FastAPI TestClient). │ ├── frontend/ ← React + TS + Vite. Empty until phase 07. │ ├── data/ ← Seed data: sample policies, claims, rules. │ Used to bootstrap the SQLite DB on first run. │ ├── docs/ │ ├── decisions.md ← Running log. Already seeded with 4 decisions: │ │ stack choice, multi-chat strategy, layer purity, │ │ "rules as data" choice. Append-only as we go. │ ├── domain-model.md ← Skeleton with placeholders. Gets filled in phase 03. │ └── self-review.md ← Stub. Written near the end (phase 09). │ ├── ai-artifacts/ ← Empty for now. Raw JSONL session logs copied here │ └── README.md at the end of the project. README explains the │ naming convention. │ ├── AGENTS.md ← Single source of truth for any new chat. ├── README.md ← Setup + run instructions for the reviewer. ├── pyproject.toml ← uv-managed Python project (deps empty for now). ├── .python-version ← Pins Python 3.11. └── .gitignore ← Python + Node + OS + DB + secrets. ``` **Two layer-purity rules baked into the structure** (also documented in `AGENTS.md`): 1. `app/domain/` and `app/adjudication/` must **never import** from `app/persistence/` or `app/api/`. Forces clean separation. Makes domain tests trivial. 2. `app/persistence/` and `app/api/` are thin adapters — they translate, they don't decide. --- ## 2. How `AGENTS.md` rules are wired up | | Mechanism | |---|---| | **Where rules live** | One file: `claim-evaluator-bot/AGENTS.md`. 319 lines. Self-contained. | | **What's in it** | Project layer on top (context, stack, conventions, phase tracker, the "read this first" list) + your general rules pasted in below. | | **Why two layers in one file** | Cursor auto-loads only the AGENTS.md at the **current workspace root**. Your `new-here/AGENTS.md` does *not* apply here. So we inline both. The file explicitly states: *"project-specific guidance wins on conflict."* | | **What enforces "read this first"** | The top section of `AGENTS.md` literally tells the agent: read `AGENTS.md` + `docs/decisions.md` + `docs/domain-model.md` + last 20 commits before doing anything. | | **Your one-liner for new chats** | Baked into AGENTS.md so you can copy it once and reuse:
`"Read AGENTS.md, docs/decisions.md, docs/domain-model.md, and git log --oneline -20. Then: ."` | | **Phase tracker** | A checkbox list inside AGENTS.md. Phase 01 (framing) marked done. We tick off the rest as we go — new chats see this and immediately know where we are. | The flow across chats looks like: ```text You open new chat for phase N │ ├─→ Cursor auto-loads AGENTS.md (workspace root → always loaded) ├─→ Top of AGENTS.md tells the agent: read decisions.md + domain-model.md + git log ├─→ Agent now has the full project context, decisions trail, current model, recent commits └─→ You paste the one-liner with today's specific task. Agent gets to work. ``` Net effect: **each chat starts cold (clean JSONL) but instantly informed (via persistent artifacts).** That's the coherence layer. --- ## 3. README — updated to match reality Three changes from the version that was sitting there: | Before | After | Why | |---|---|---| | `🏗️ Foundation only.` | `Foundation only.` (no emoji) | Your AGENTS.md prohibits emojis | | Tree showed `pyproject.toml` and `.python-version` as if normal | Tree shows them, plus a `# Added in later phases (not present yet)` comment block for files that don't exist | Don't lie to the reviewer about what's in the repo | | Tree was missing `app/tests/api/` | Added it | Match reality | | "uv sync currently installs nothing because deps haven't been added yet" | "Neither command does anything useful yet — this repo is currently foundation only" | More honest, less hand-wavy | Read the full thing here if you want: ```12:50:/Users/adagrewal/Desktop/Sumer_stuff/claim-evaluator-bot/README.md ## Status Foundation only — folder skeleton, docs scaffolding, and agent guidance are in place. No application code yet. See the [phase tracker in `AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md#phase-tracker) for progress. ## Stack | Layer | Choice | |---|---| | Backend | Python 3.11+, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, Pydantic | | Package manager | [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) | | Persistence | SQLite (file-based, zero external setup) | | Backend testing | `pytest` + `httpx` | | Frontend | React + TypeScript, Vite | | Frontend testing | Vitest + React Testing Library (if time permits) | ``` --- ## What's NOT in scope of "foundation" I deliberately stopped short of: - `uv add fastapi sqlalchemy ...` → no real dependencies installed yet (deps are listed as comments in `pyproject.toml`).
